turns.content: I'm so frustrated, I just spent hours trying to understand why my for loop keeps breaking when I try to split a huge vector into smaller pieces instead of processing everything at once. Why does the standard iteration approach even feel wrong here?

That is actually a really common hurdle! You're hitting it because Rust's iterators are lazy, meaning they don't build a new list in memory until you ask them to. For your batching problem, the crate itertools has a helper called chunks that takes an iterator and a size parameter to yield fixed-size batches directly. It handles the remainder items automatically by creating extra empty batches or padding depending on your needs.
Oh okay so I don't need to write my own loop logic? Can you show me exactly how to chain chunks with a closure that does something for each batch?

Sure, you can just call the iterator method on your vector like this: vec_data.chunks(5).into_iter().for_each(|batch| { /* process items in this batch */ }). It returns an Iterator trait implementation that yields each chunk as a slice. If you need to group by another field instead of fixed size, itertools also has chunk_by which takes a key function to determine where one group ends and the next begins.

I've read about itertools::chunks but I still don't fully understand how it handles remainders when the total count isn't a perfect multiple of the batch size. My Java background makes me assume that if I have 5 items and want chunks of 2, I'll just get two full batches and ignore the leftover, but in Rust's iterator model it seems like the remainder needs to be handled explicitly somewhere.

You are correct that Java's Stream API often swallows remainders or requires explicit filtering, whereas Rust's itertools::chunks iterates over every single item including the partial batch at the end. The key difference is that chunks doesn't drop items; instead it yields a final iterator with fewer elements if there isn't enough to fill another complete batch. For example, iterating over 5 items with a chunk size of 2 will produce three iterators: one with two items, another with two items, and a third containing just the single remaining item.

Ah okay so I don't need to manually filter or collect remainders separately. But what if I want to process only full batches and skip that final partial chunk? Does itertools::chunks have a built-in way to do this without writing my own loop logic?

That is exactly the common mistake beginners make by assuming there's a single filter method on chunks itself. Actually, itertools doesn't provide a direct 'skip remainder' variant; you must use the iterator protocol to collect full batches only. You can write a simple loop that collects items into a buffer and yields it only when the buffer reaches the chunk size, then clears it for the next batch. If you want to skip the final partial batch entirely, you should check if the collected buffer length equals your desired batch size before yielding it.

I'm back after five years, trying to build a script to process large log files in batches. I thought using itertools::chunks would just split the iterator into fixed-size groups like I expected, but it seems like my logic for handling the last partial batch is broken.

That's a common misunderstanding when coming back to Rust iterators. The chunks function actually returns an iterator over iterators, not the data itself, and you need to collect each inner iterator into a vector before processing it. Also, if your size isn't evenly divisible by the batch count, the final chunk might be incomplete depending on how you iterate through the outer results.

You're right about the double iteration, but I'm still confused why my code crashes when there's a remainder. I thought chunks would just give me an empty iterator for leftovers instead of failing or skipping them entirely like in other languages.

The issue is that chunks doesn't automatically handle remainders gracefully; you need to explicitly collect the results using a closure or map function. The key insight is that Rust iterators don't support partial consumption without explicit bounds checking, so if you want to preserve remainders, you should use chunk_by with a custom predicate or manually track position counters instead of relying on automatic splitting.
